name: Cipher Digital Inc.

profile.description: Cipher Digital Inc. (formerly Cipher Mining) develops and operates industrial-scale data centers for Bitcoin mining and high-performance computing. The company has secured approximately $11.4 billion in contracted HPC/AI data center revenue over 10-15 year lease terms with hyperscalers including Google, AWS, and Fluidstack across 907 MW of operating and contracted capacity.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Innovation Track Record (2/3): Cipher executed one of the most successful Bitcoin-miner-to-HPC pivots in the industry, rebranding to Cipher Digital in February 2026 and securing $11.4B in contracted revenue with Google, AWS, and Fluidstack — up from $9.3B just one quarter ago [1][2]. AI Deployment (1/2): The company's 907 MW of operating and contracted capacity directly serves AI/HPC workloads, though it provides infrastructure rather than AI products — Barber Lake facility nearing substantial completion for first meaningful HPC revenue later in 2026 [1]. Market Share Leadership (1/2): Among the leading Bitcoin-to-HPC converters alongside Core Scientific, with 600 MW contracted to tier-1 hyperscalers, though the broader data center market is dominated by Equinix and Digital Realty [2]. Product Pipeline (2/2): $11.4B contracted backlog over 10-15 year terms provides exceptional revenue visibility; CEO Page characterizes 2026 as the 'year of execution' with specific facility completion timelines [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): No evidence of above-average R&D spending; Cipher's model is construction and operations, not technology development.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Cipher's stated purpose centers on 'developing industrial-scale data centers engineered for next-generation compute at the highest standards of innovation, precision, and excellence' — clear and functional but generic infrastructure language without a deeper purpose [3]. Community Impact (0/2): 74% renewable energy utilization at Texas sites is notable for the industry but no charitable programs, community investment, or foundation activity was identified [4]. Employee Retention (0/2): Only 66 employees; recently added industry veterans Lee Bratcher and Drew for AI/HPC leadership, but no retention metrics available for such a small team [3]. Insider Buying (0/2): Significant insider selling — CEO Page sold $604K in shares in March 2026, directors Grossman ($436K) and Williams ($627K) sold in May 2026, and multiple officers exercised and sold [5].

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically calculated from available metrics. D/E of 641.6% scores 0.0 (exceeds 300% floor); ROE of -121.7% scores 0.0 (2x weight); revenue growth YoY of -28.8% scores 0.0 (quarterly decline as mining winds down); revenue multi-year CAGR of ~32.8% scores 1.0 (2x weight) reflecting explosive growth from near-zero base; forward P/E of 43.7 scores 0.41; profit margin of 0% scores 0.0 [6]. The extreme D/E reflects $2.77B total debt from bond offerings to fund data center construction against $806M equity. Q1 2026 net loss was $114M ($0.28/share), improving from Q4 2025's $734M loss [1]. Liquidity passes: $20.55 x 26.3M avg vol = $540M daily.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9 — IMMUNE): Cipher Digital IS AI infrastructure — the company builds and operates the physical data centers where AI workloads run [1][2]. AI cannot disrupt building physical power infrastructure, cooling systems, and compute facilities; rather, AI growth directly increases demand for Cipher's product. The $11.4B in take-or-pay contracts with Google and AWS provide revenue certainty regardless of which AI models or frameworks prevail. Physical data center construction has high barriers to entry (power procurement, permitting, construction expertise) that AI cannot replicate [1]. AI Opportunity (1/1): Cipher's entire forward revenue model is AI-driven — 907 MW of capacity contracted specifically for HPC/AI workloads with hyperscalers, representing direct and substantial AI revenue exposure [1][2].

infinite_game.summary: Cipher demonstrates meaningful infinite-game qualities through its transformative pivot from Bitcoin mining to AI data center infrastructure, secured by 10-15 year contracts that force long-term thinking. CEO Tyler Page's willingness to abandon the profitable mining business for a fundamentally different model shows existential flexibility. However, the company is only four years old, the leadership has not been tested through adversity beyond the pivot itself, and heavy insider selling suggests some finite-game thinking at the individual level.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Cipher's evolution from Bitcoin mining to 'powering next-generation compute' represents a genuine purpose that connects its infrastructure work to the broader AI revolution. The 10-15 year contract structures embed long-term thinking into the business model. However, the purpose appears driven primarily by economic opportunity (HPC margins far exceed mining) rather than a transcendent vision of what the world should look like [3].

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): With only 66 employees managing multi-billion-dollar construction projects, Cipher operates as a lean, specialized team. The recent addition of industry veterans Lee Bratcher and Drew for AI and HPC leadership, plus an orderly CFO transition from Ed Farrell to Greg Mumford, suggests competent team management [3]. However, the team's small size makes it untestable by Sinek's standards — there is insufficient evidence of organizational culture at scale.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): No evidence of an infinite competitive mindset. Cipher competes with Core Scientific, Applied Digital, and traditional data center operators for hyperscaler contracts. The company's competitive language focuses on its own 'best-in-class' capabilities rather than learning from rivals' strengths.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): Cipher executed a textbook transformation — abandoning its core Bitcoin mining business (winding down Black Pearl operations), rebranding from Cipher Mining to Cipher Digital, and rebuilding entirely around HPC/AI data center hosting [1][2]. This is precisely the kind of strategic pivot Sinek celebrates: recognizing that the world changed, willingly destroying the existing business model, and committing fully to a new one. The $11.4B contracted backlog validates the pivot's success.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Raising $2.77B in bonds to fund data center construction while mining revenue winds down requires significant conviction. CEO Page committed to calling 2026 the 'year of execution,' setting clear expectations rather than overpromising [1]. However, the simultaneous insider selling by CEO and directors ($1.6M+ combined in recent months) undermines the narrative of all-in leadership conviction [5].

investment_thesis: Cipher Digital has increased its contracted HPC revenue backlog to $11.4B over 10-15 year terms (up from $9.3B last quarter), with 907 MW of operating and contracted capacity across Google, AWS, and Fluidstack [1]. The Barber Lake facility is approaching substantial completion, which will trigger the first meaningful HPC revenue recognition in H2 2026, with analyst consensus projecting $835M revenue by fiscal 2027 and profitability reaching $0.47 forward EPS [1][6]. At $20.55/share ($8.4B market cap), Cipher trades at just 0.74x its contracted revenue backlog, and Morgan Stanley's Overweight rating at $38 implies 85% upside. The 74% renewable energy mix at Texas sites positions the company well for ESG-conscious hyperscaler customers [4].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on extreme leverage: $2.77B total debt against $806M stockholders' equity (641% D/E) creates significant financial risk if construction timelines slip or interest rates rise [6]. Q1 2026 revenue declined to $35M (from $60M in Q4 2025) as mining winds down, creating a revenue trough before HPC income commences — the company reported a $114M net loss for the quarter [1]. Insider selling by the CEO ($604K), President ($533K), and directors ($1.06M) in March-May 2026 raises concerns about management conviction despite bullish public messaging [5]. Customer concentration is extreme — two hyperscaler relationships drive the entire $11.4B backlog, and any contract modification would be devastating. Construction delays, power procurement issues, or cost overruns on simultaneous multi-hundred-MW builds could strain the $754M liquidity position.
key_catalysts: Barber Lake facility reaching substantial completion and first HPC revenue recognition in H2 2026, transforming Cipher from a money-losing miner to a contracted data center operator
Additional hyperscaler contract announcements beyond the current 907 MW, which would drive further backlog growth and re-rating toward traditional data center multiples
Full-year 2027 revenue inflection to projected $835M as both Barber Lake and Black Pearl facilities reach full commercial operation
key_risks: Construction execution risk — simultaneous multi-hundred-MW builds with only 66 employees; delays at Barber Lake or Black Pearl defer revenue recognition and pressure liquidity
Extreme leverage at 641% D/E with $2.77B bonds; rising interest rates or credit downgrades could compress equity value even if operations perform
Customer concentration — Google and AWS drive essentially all of the $11.4B contracted backlog; loss or renegotiation of either relationship would be catastrophic to the equity
